title: Selecting the Proper Method of Magnetization
narrationScript: Up to now you have learned the individual tools of magnetic particle testing. Heads, coils, prods, yokes, central conductors, alternating current, half wave, full wave. A Level Two earns the title by choosing among them. Hand a Level Two a part they have never seen, and within a few minutes they should produce a defensible plan covering method, directions, current, amperage, sequence, and proof that the field was right. This lesson builds that habit around five questions. Question one. What is the part. Alloy, shape, and condition. The alloy sets the magnetic personality. A soft, low carbon steel takes flux easily but forgets it the moment current stops, so it must be inspected by the continuous method, with particles applied while the current is on. A hardened, high carbon or alloy steel holds a strong residual field. That opens the door to the residual method where the procedure permits it, and it also means the part will need real demagnetization afterward. Heat treatment matters too. The same bar behaves differently annealed and hardened. Shape decides the hardware. A bore or a ring wants a central conductor, so the inside surface gets a field without any electrical contact. A long bar suits a coil, and the coil rule of thumb works between a length to diameter ratio of about two and fifteen, so a short, chunky part needs stacking or pole extenders, and a long part is done in sections of about eighteen inches. Condition closes the question. Hot, rough, as welded surfaces favor dry powder. Smooth machined surfaces favor the wet fluorescent bath. A finished bearing journal or any aerospace surface must never see an arc strike, which rules out prods, and direct contact gets clean pads and care, or you avoid contact entirely with a coil or a central conductor. Question two. What current. Alternating current rides the skin of the part. It concentrates the field at the surface, shakes the particles so they migrate to leakage fields, and excels at the fatigue cracks that start at the surface of service parts. It will not find anything deep. Half wave direct current is the field workhorse. It pulses, so it keeps particle mobility, and it pushes the field below the surface, which is why it pairs so well with dry powder on welds. Full wave direct current penetrates deepest and runs on bench units with the wet method. Remember that a deep full wave residual field is also the hardest one to remove later. Question three. Which directions. Flux must cross a discontinuity to leak, and detection is reliable when the angle between flux and flaw is roughly forty five to ninety degrees. Current through or around the part makes a circular field that finds flaws lying along the part. Flux along the part, from a coil or a yoke, finds flaws lying across it. Since you rarely know the orientation in advance, full coverage means two magnetizations roughly at right angles, each inspected separately. Question four. What sequence. The continuous method is the default and the most sensitive. Flood the surface, let the flow taper off, then fire the shot while the particles can still move. Two short shots of about half a second are typical. The residual method, magnetize first and bathe after, is allowed only on retentive parts and only when the procedure says so. Sequence also means the order of directions. Each new shot overwrites the last field, so finish one direction, record what you found, then magnetize the other way. On retentive parts, when the first field was the stronger one, demagnetize between directions so the leftover field cannot distort the second shot. Question five. How much field. Start with the rules of thumb. On the order of three hundred to eight hundred amperes per inch of diameter for a direct head shot. Ampere turns near forty five thousand divided by length over diameter for a low fill factor coil. Ninety to one hundred twenty five amperes per inch of spacing for prods. Treat every one of those as a starting point, because the numbers in your written procedure govern. Then prove the field. A shim with an artificial flaw should light up clearly. A Hall effect meter reading a tangential field on the order of thirty to sixty gauss is a common target. Too little field and fine cracks stay invisible. Too much and the surface furs over with false background that buries real indications. Ask the five questions in order, write down the answers, and you have a technique you can defend to any auditor. That is the difference between running a machine and engineering an inspection.
imagePrompt: A technician studies a splined steel shaft clamped between the copper-braid contact pads of a wet horizontal magnetic particle bench, one hand on the amperage control, a dark UV inspection curtain drawn behind the unit.

sections.heading: The Level II's Job: Build the Plan
sections.body:
A Level I runs the technique that is handed down. A Level II builds it. When an unfamiliar part hits the bench, the Level II answers five questions, in order, before any current flows:

- **What is the part?** Alloy, shape, and condition.
- **What current type?** AC, half-wave DC, or full-wave DC.
- **Which field directions?** Circular, longitudinal, or both.
- **What sequence?** Continuous or residual, and the order of shots.
- **How much field?** Enough flux density to find flaws without burying them.

Every answer comes from the part and the governing procedure, not from habit. The sections below take the questions one at a time, and the workshop lesson at the end of this module turns them into complete written plans for three real parts. Answer all five and you have a technique you can defend; skip one and you are guessing.

sections.heading: Alloy, Shape, and Condition of the Part
sections.body:
**Alloy** sets the magnetic personality. Soft, low-carbon steels have high permeability and low retentivity: they accept flux easily and forget it the instant current stops, so they must be inspected by the **continuous method**. Hardened, high-carbon and alloy steels — bearing races, quench-and-tempered 4140 or 4340, tool steels — hold a strong residual field, because the same metallurgy that resists dislocation movement also pins domains. High retentivity opens the door to the **residual method** where the procedure allows it, and it guarantees the part will need real demagnetization afterward. Heat treatment changes the answer: the same bar is magnetically soft annealed and magnetically hard after hardening.

**Shape** picks the hardware. Bores, tubes, and rings call for a **central conductor**, which magnetizes the inside surface with no electrical contact. Long, straight parts suit a **coil**; the classic low-fill-factor rule works for length-to-diameter ratios of roughly 2 to 15, so short, chunky parts need stacking or pole extenders, and long parts are magnetized in sections of about 18 inches. Complex geometry means more shots, not more amperage.

**Condition** finishes the call. Hot, rough, as-welded surfaces favor dry powder (some rated to about 600 °F) with HWDC. Smooth machined surfaces favor the wet fluorescent bath. A finished bearing journal or any aerospace surface must never see an arc strike — prods are widely banned there, and direct-contact head shots need clean pads and care, or you avoid contact entirely with a coil or central conductor.

sections.caption: Five decisions turn an unfamiliar part into a written technique: know the part, choose field direction(s), choose current type, set the sequence, and verify the flux density.

sections.heading: Type of Magnetizing Current
sections.body:
Current type is a depth-and-mobility decision.

- **AC** reverses continuously, and the skin effect concentrates its field in the outer skin of the part. It finds **surface flaws only** — but for them it is excellent, because the alternating field vibrates particles and walks them toward leakage fields. Service-induced fatigue cracks, which start at the surface, are classic AC work.
- **Half-wave DC (HWDC)** is rectified AC with the reverse half removed: a pulsating, one-direction current. It penetrates below the surface while keeping strong particle mobility, and it is the field favorite with **dry powder on welds**, where slag lines and lack of fusion can sit just under the surface.
- **Full-wave DC (FWDC)** gives the deepest penetration and the steadiest field. It is the standard on bench units with the wet method. The price comes later: a deep FWDC residual field is the hardest to demagnetize, and AC demagnetizing equipment cannot reach it.

Match the current to where the flaws live. Chasing subsurface lack of fusion with AC finds nothing; flooding a surface-fatigue inspection with maximum FWDC just trades sensitivity for background.

sections.heading: Direction of the Magnetic Field
sections.body:
A discontinuity shows only when flux **crosses** it and is forced to leak. Detection is reliable when the angle between the flux lines and the flaw is roughly **45 to 90 degrees**; a flaw lying parallel to the flux barely disturbs it and stays invisible.

- **Circular magnetization** — current through the part (head shot, prods) or through a central conductor — wraps flux around the part and finds flaws **parallel to the current path**: seams, laps, and cracks running along a shaft's axis.
- **Longitudinal magnetization** — a coil, cable wrap, or yoke — sends flux along the part and finds **transverse** flaws lying across it.

You rarely know flaw orientation in advance. Shafts crack along grain flow and across fillets; welds crack both along and across the bead. So full coverage means **two magnetizations roughly 90° apart**, each applied, bathed, and inspected as its own operation. One shot is half an inspection, whatever the amperage.

sections.caption: A circular field catches flaws lying along the current path; a longitudinal field catches transverse flaws — full coverage takes both.

sections.heading: Sequence of Operations
sections.body:
**Continuous or residual.** The continuous method — particles applied while the magnetizing force is on — is the default and the more sensitive, because the surface field is at its maximum when the particles arrive. On a wet bench the rhythm is: flood the surface, divert the bath, then fire while the film is still mobile — two shots of about a half second are typical. With dry powder: current on, dust a light cloud, gently blow off the excess, current off. The residual method — magnetize first, apply particles after — is permitted only on high-retentivity parts and only when the procedure explicitly allows it.

**Order of directions.** Each new magnetization overwrites the previous field, so finish one direction completely — shoot, bathe, inspect, record — before magnetizing the other way. When the first field is stronger than the second (a heavy coil shot followed by a lighter circular shot on a retentive part, for example), demagnetize between directions so the leftover field cannot tilt or weaken the second one.

**Everything else in order:** verify bath concentration and lighting before the first shot, clean and demagnetize after the last one, and document as you go. Sequence errors do not look like errors — they look like clean parts.

sections.heading: Value of Flux Density
sections.body:
Amperage rules of thumb get you near the right flux density; verification proves you arrived.

Typical procedure starting points (your written procedure governs):

- **Head shot:** on the order of 300–800 A per inch of part diameter; low-alloy steels commonly start near the middle of that band.
- **Coil (low fill factor):** ampere-turns ≈ 45,000 ÷ (L/D), effective for L/D between about 2 and 15.
- **Prods:** about 90–125 A per inch of prod spacing, with spacing commonly 6–8 inches.

Then prove the field, because the formulas know nothing about your part's exact permeability, geometry, or section changes:

- A **QQI shim** (artificial-flaw standard) taped flaw-side-down should produce a clear indication at technique amperage.
- A **pie gauge** gives a quick read of field direction on the surface.
- A **Hall-effect gaussmeter** reading a tangential surface field on the order of **30–60 gauss** is a common procedure target.

Both directions of error cost you. Too little flux and fine, tight cracks never leak enough to hold particles. Too much and the surface 'furs' with feathery background, particles pile at section changes and sharp corners, and real indications drown in noise. When the QQI shows up clean against a quiet background, the flux density is right — and that is the reading worth recording.

sections.title: Five answers, on paper, every time
sections.body: Part (**alloy · shape · condition**) → **directions** (two, ≈90° apart) → **current type** (AC / HWDC / FWDC, by flaw depth) → **sequence** (continuous unless residual is justified and permitted) → **flux density** (formula starting point, **QQI or gaussmeter proof**). If any one of the five is missing from a technique sheet, the inspection is not ready to run.

checkQuestions.question: A hardened 52100 bearing race and a low-carbon structural bracket both need MT. Which statement about method eligibility is correct?
options: Both parts may use the residual method if it saves bench time
The race (high retentivity) may qualify for the residual method where the procedure permits; the bracket (low retentivity) must use the continuous method
The bracket may use the residual method because soft steel magnetizes more easily
Neither part may use the continuous method
checkQuestions.explanation: The residual method depends on the part holding a strong field after current stops. Hardened high-carbon alloys have high retentivity and may qualify; low-carbon steel forgets its field immediately, so it must be inspected with particles applied while current flows.

checkQuestions.question: Lack of fusion is suspected just below the surface of a rough, as-welded joint being inspected in the field with dry powder. The best current choice is:
options: AC, because the skin effect drives the field deeper into the weld
Full-wave DC, because dry powder requires it
Half-wave DC — a pulsating current with subsurface penetration and strong dry-particle mobility
Any current type — the particles, not the current, determine detection depth
checkQuestions.explanation: HWDC penetrates below the surface while its pulsations keep dry powder mobile, which is why it is the standard pairing for weld inspection with dry particles. AC's skin effect limits it to surface flaws.

checkQuestions.question: Transverse fatigue cracks are expected across the journal of a pump shaft. Which magnetization puts flux at the best angle to them?
options: A longitudinal field from a coil — flux runs along the shaft and crosses transverse cracks at roughly 90°
A circular field from a head shot — current along the shaft finds cracks across it
Either direction — a strong enough field finds any orientation
A circular field, because fatigue cracks always follow the current path
checkQuestions.explanation: Detection needs flux crossing the flaw at about 45–90°. A coil drives flux along the shaft axis, which intersects transverse cracks at right angles; a circular field would run parallel to them and miss.

checkQuestions.question: A 10-inch-long, 2-inch-diameter bar is magnetized in a 5-turn, low-fill-factor coil. Using NI ≈ 45,000/(L/D), the starting current is about:
checkQuestions.explanation: L/D = 10/2 = 5, so NI ≈ 45,000/5 = 9,000 ampere-turns. With 5 turns, I ≈ 9,000/5 = 1,800 A — a starting value to be verified with a QQI or gaussmeter per the procedure.

checkQuestions.question: During a two-direction bench inspection of a retentive shaft, the correct sequence is:
options: Fire both shots back-to-back, then bathe and inspect once
Apply particles only after both fields are off, because the residual method is more sensitive
Inspect the second direction first, since its field is freshest
Bathe and inspect after the first direction, record results, then magnetize the second direction — demagnetizing between shots if the first field was the stronger
checkQuestions.explanation: Each magnetization overwrites the previous field, so every direction must be applied, bathed, inspected, and recorded as its own operation. On retentive parts a stronger leftover field can distort the next shot, so demag between directions when the procedure or field strengths call for it.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §2.6 (selecting the proper method of magnetization)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §2.6.1–2.6.5 (alloy/shape/condition, current type, field direction, sequence of operations, flux density)
Practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V Article 7 address technique selection and field verification (generic reference)
Reference values: coil rule NI ≈ 45,000/(L/D); prods ≈ 90–125 A/in of spacing; tangential field ≈ 30–60 G
